lines.text: Agents are getting good at generating business workflows. But capability isn't the hard part — governance is. Who decides when an agent's action is clear enough, safe enough, and authorized enough to actually ship? ClarityLoop is that layer. The model proposes; deterministic code decides.

Take an ambiguous request — same as last time, a hundred and twenty cartons, supplier quote attached. We run ClarityLoop, streaming live from Alibaba Cloud. It keeps a structured state and scores operational entropy deterministically — the model never invents a number. The loop gathers the next best evidence, and the commit residual falls. Once the authority boundary is clear and the evidence is sufficient, the gate clears it to commit.

Does it actually work? On thirty-six cases, with one uniform scorer across five baselines, a capability-only agent commits unsafe actions thirty-six percent of the time. ClarityLoop drives that to zero, while keeping eighty-six percent task completion. And because the fixed gate and ClarityLoop share the same predicate, that completion gain is provably the evidence loop.

Workflows improve over time. ClarityLoop promotes a new version only when replay shows it is strictly safer than the one already in production — safety-weighted regression gating, with full version lineage.

And it composes. Harness evolution raises task success — capability. ClarityLoop drives attack-success to zero — safety. On the AgentDojo injection benchmark, the gate blocks unauthorized tool calls before they execute. Wrap any agent: keep its capability, remove its unsafe commits. Harnesses evolve. ClarityLoop decides what ships.
